mission.title: Install and Configure Score on Kubernetes
mission.description: The Score Specification provides a developer-centric and platform-agnostic workload specification to improve developer productivity and experience. Installing Score allows you to define and manage your workloads in a consistent manner across different environments.

steps.title: Add the Score Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official Score Helm repository to your Helm configuration.
```bash
helm repo add score https://charts.score.dev
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Score using Helm
steps.description:
Install the Score chart into your Kubernetes cluster. This command will create a namespace called 'score' if it doesn't exist.
```bash
helm install score score/score --version 0.3.0 --namespace score --create-namespace
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the Score pods are running in the 'score' namespace.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace score
```

steps.title: Post-install configuration
steps.description:
You may want to configure resource limits for the Score deployment. Edit the deployment as follows:
```bash
kubectl edit deployment score --namespace score
```
Add resource limits under the containers section:
```yaml
resources:
  limits:
    cpu: "500m"
    memory: "512Mi"
```

steps.title: Access the Score service
steps.description:
To access the Score service, you can port-forward the service to your local machine:
```bash
kubectl port-forward service/score --namespace score 8080:80
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation will have the Score pods running in the 'score' namespace, and you will be able to access the service via port-forwarding. You can also verify that the resource limits have been applied correctly.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add score https://charts.score.dev
helm repo update

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Score Helm release from your Kubernetes cluster using the following command. This will remove the deployment and associated resources but not CRDs or persistent volumes.
```bash
helm uninstall score --namespace score
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
If you have created any custom resource definitions (CRDs) or persistent volume claims (PVCs) that are no longer needed, you can delete them with the following commands. Replace 'score' with your specific CRD name if necessary.
```bash
kubectl delete crd scores.score.dev
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace score
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Finally, delete the 'score' namespace to clean up any remaining resources. Verify that the namespace has been removed.
```bash
kubectl delete namespace score
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, it's a good practice to back up your current Helm release configuration. You can do this by exporting the current values.
```bash
helm get values score --namespace score > score-backup-values.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and upgrade the Score release to the latest version. Ensure you specify the correct version if needed.
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade score score/score --version 0.4.0 --namespace score
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check that the Score pods are running the new version in the 'score' namespace. You can also check the release history for confirmation.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace score
helm history score --namespace score
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If your Score pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, you can check the logs to diagnose the issue. Use the following command to view the logs of a specific pod:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace score
```

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If you cannot access the Score service, ensure that the service is running and check the port-forwarding command. Verify the service status with:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace score
```

troubleshooting.title: Helm upgrade fails with version conflict
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter a version conflict during the Helm upgrade, ensure that you are using a compatible version of the Score chart. You can check the available versions with:
```bash
helm search repo score --versions
```

troubleshooting.title: Insufficient resources for Score pods
troubleshooting.description:
If the Score pods are failing to start due to insufficient resources, you may need to adjust the resource limits in the deployment. Check the events for the pods to see resource-related errors:
```bash
kubectl describe pod <pod-name> --namespace score
```

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a running Kubernetes cluster and have Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
